Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Results explicitly state ATC knockdown was performed for ATC (At3g20330).

Source_Species.reason: The study investigates Arabidopsis ATC throughout, including Arabidopsis lines and recombinant Arabidopsis protein.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Methods and main text explicitly name Arabidopsis thaliana.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Wild-type/ecotype used is Col-0, stated in Fig. 1 and Methods.

Applied_Species.reason: The applied organism for plant experiments is Arabidopsis.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is explicitly used for the transgenic and phenotypic analyses.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The plant lines are in Col-0 background, stated in figure legend and Methods.

Enzyme_Name.reason: ATC is explicitly defined as aspartate transcarbamoylase.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number is given anywhere in the provided paper text.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper states ATC preserves the overall architecture of the transcarbamoylase family, forms a catalytic homotrimer, has an N-terminal chloroplast transit peptide, and contains CP-loop and Asp-loop.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: ATC is explicitly described as an essential enzyme for de novo pyrimidine biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The text states the pathway starts in the chloroplast and ATC is the major regulated enzyme for de novo synthesis of UMP in plants; this supports entry/committed step of plastidial UMP biosynthesis.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: ATC catalyzes the pathway-starting condensation reaction, supporting an upstream/committed position.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper explicitly says ATC is the major regulated enzyme for de novo synthesis of UMP in plants and a key function in growth regulation.

Primary_Substrate.reason: ATC is explicitly said to use aspartate and carbamoyl phosphate.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper states ATC catalyzes condensation to form carbamoyl aspartate.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This reaction description matches the introductory description of ATC as the first chloroplast/plastid step in de novo pyrimidine synthesis.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The text states UMP is produced downstream and is the precursor of all pyrimidine nucleotides; ATC initiates that pathway.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: UMP is explicitly identified as the end product precursor and feedback inhibitor connected to ATC.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: UMP is explicitly described as a pyrimidine nucleotide/precursor of pyrimidine nucleotides.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The introduction explicitly states pyrimidine nucleotides are components of nucleic acids and cofactors in synthesis of sugars, polysaccharides, glycoproteins, and phospholipids; UMP is the precursor of all pyrimidine nucleotides.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: The discussion cites prior studies showing transcriptional regulation by tissue pyrimidine availability and TOR pathway, but this was not directly tested in Results of this paper.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The paper experimentally altered ATC transcript and protein levels in Arabidopsis lines and measured them in leaf material; however 'leaf-expressed' itself is only indirectly implied from leaf assays.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The introduction states the pathway starts in the chloroplast, ATC can be anchored to the inner plastid membrane, and the protein has an N-terminal chloroplast transit peptide.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show ATC knockdown strongly reduces growth and photosynthetic efficiency, overexpression enhances growth, and biochemical/structural data show feedback inhibition by UMP.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are explicitly described and used: amiRNA knockdown, overexpression, qPCR, immunoblotting, growth/PAM phenotyping, kinetics, ITC, crystallography, PALA inhibition, and mutagenesis.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The paper explicitly reports growth conditions, exogenous PALA treatment, and biochemical assays with UMP, CP, and Asp.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods state 35S-CaMV promoter for amiRNA construct and ubiquitin-10 promoter for overexpression.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Severe growth reduction, chlorosis, and reduced photosynthetic efficiency are directly shown for downregulation; proposed constitutive activation tradeoffs were indeed not tested, which is accurately framed.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Discussion explicitly proposes ATC as herbicide target, overexpression to enhance growth, and UMP-insensitive variants for transgenic crop improvement.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Results and Discussion explicitly show plant ATC is feedback-inhibited by UMP at the active site, forms a homotrimer, and supports sequential firing with one subunit closed at a time.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four summary points are directly supported by the Results and Discussion: growth/photosynthesis effects, active-site UMP inhibition, overexpression/knockdown phenotypes, and structural evidence for subunit communication and sequential firing.

corrections.reason: No EC number is given anywhere in the provided paper text.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: The mutant is derived from Arabidopsis ATC, whose gene is At3g20330, but the exact field value 'At3g20330 (F161A mutant protein)' does not appear verbatim in the paper.

Source_Species.reason: F161A is a mutant of Arabidopsis ATC.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is the source organism for the ATC sequence used to generate F161A.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: The mutant protein is derived from Arabidopsis ATC used in this study, likely Col-0-derived cDNA, but the paper does not explicitly say 'Derived from Col-0 ATC' for the recombinant construct.

Applied_Species.reason: The mutant was analyzed as recombinant protein produced in E. coli, but the field label 'Arabidopsis recombinant protein' is not a species designation used in the paper.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The protein sequence is from Arabidopsis thaliana and expression host is E. coli BLR(DE3)-pLysS, but the exact combined value 'Arabidopsis thaliana / Escherichia coli' is not stated as an applied species field.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper explicitly refers to mutant F161A of Arabidopsis ATC, i.e., an aspartate transcarbamoylase variant.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number is provided in the paper.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: F161A is explicitly a CP-loop mutation in Arabidopsis ATC, which belongs to the transcarbamoylase family.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: ATC/F161A pertains to de novo pyrimidine biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: As for ATC, the enzyme acts at the plastidial entry step toward UMP biosynthesis.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The enzyme's position is the same upstream committed step catalyzed by ATC.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: F161A specifically alters the regulatory control of ATC, which the paper identifies as a major regulated enzyme/key control point.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Kinetic assays with F161A use CP and Asp as substrates, implying same substrates as WT.

Primary_Product.reason: As the ATC mutant, it catalyzes the same reaction producing carbamoyl aspartate; this follows from the enzyme identity and activity assays.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Results show F161A retains catalytic activity similar to WT but with altered regulation, supporting the same condensation reaction with altered control.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Because F161A is an ATC variant in the UMP biosynthetic pathway and is not inhibited by UMP, it still supplies precursor pool toward UMP while escaping feedback inhibition.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: UMP is explicitly the feedback inhibitor/end product connected to the mutant phenotype.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: UMP is explicitly identified as precursor of all pyrimidine nucleotides.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: F161A was generated and analyzed as recombinant protein in vitro; no in planta expression pattern was studied.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results explicitly show F161A is not inhibited by UMP, has similar catalytic activity to WT, higher affinity for CP and PALA, and in crystals binds three PALAs per trimer.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The mutant was studied by site-directed mutagenesis, kinetics, ITC, and X-ray crystallography.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The mutant was assayed with UMP, PALA, CP, and Asp in vitro.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results explicitly show substrate inhibition at high substrate concentrations for F161A, unlike WT, supporting the proposed coordination tradeoff.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Discussion explicitly proposes engineering UMP-insensitive ATC variants to enhance crop growth.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Results and Discussion explicitly assign F161 a dual role in stabilizing the inhibited conformation and preventing simultaneous activation of all three subunits; alanine removes steric hindrance around the trimer axis.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four summary points are directly supported: UMP insensitivity, near-WT activity with stronger CP/PALA binding, three PALA molecules in crystal structure, and substrate inhibition indicating altered sequential firing.
